stem: To rationalize the denominator of \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{[[a]]} - \sqrt{[[b]]}} \), you multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate \( \sqrt{[[a]]} + \sqrt{[[b]]} \). What rational number is the resulting denominator?
distractor_rationales: This adds the radicands; the difference of squares gives \( a - b \), not \( a + b \).
This reverses the subtraction, giving the negative of the correct denominator.
This is off by one from the correct denominator.
This is off by one from the correct denominator.
This is off by two from the correct denominator.

stem: To rationalize the denominator of \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{[[a]]} + [[b]]} \), you multiply numerator and denominator by the conjugate \( \sqrt{[[a]]} - [[b]] \). What rational number is the resulting denominator?
distractor_rationales: This adds \( b^{2} \) instead of subtracting it; the difference of squares gives \( a - b^{2} \).
This forgets to square \( b \) before subtracting.
This is off by one from the correct denominator.
This is off by one from the correct denominator.
This is off by two from the correct denominator.

stem: To rationalize the denominator of \( \dfrac{1}{\sqrt[3]{[[a]]}} \), you multiply numerator and denominator by \( \sqrt[3]{[[a]]^{2}} \). What integer is the resulting denominator?
solution: Multiplying gives \( \sqrt[3]{[[a]]} \cdot \sqrt[3]{[[a]]^{2}} = \sqrt[3]{[[a]]^{3}} = [[a]] = [[answer]] \), a rational (in fact integer) denominator.
distractor_rationales: This reports \( a^{2} \), the exponent inside the multiplier, not the simplified product.
This reports \( a^{3} \) instead of taking the cube root of it.
This confuses the process with multiplying by 3 instead of by \( \sqrt[3]{a^{2}} \).
This is off by one from the correct denominator.
This is off by one from the correct denominator.
This is off by two from the correct denominator.

stem: Simplify \( \sqrt{[[a]] + [[sign]]\cdot 2\sqrt{[[c]]}} \) by writing the radicand as a perfect square of a binomial in two square roots.
solution: Since \( [[a]] = [[p]]^{2} + [[q]]^{2} \) and \( [[c]] = ([[p]] \cdot [[q]])^{2} \), the radicand is \( \left([[p]] \; [[sign]] \; [[q]]\right)^{2} \) in absolute value, so \( \sqrt{[[a]] + [[sign]]\cdot 2\sqrt{[[c]]}} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This gives the sum \( p+q \) regardless of the sign inside the radical, ignoring when the sign is negative.
This gives \( |p-q| \) regardless of the sign inside the radical, ignoring when the sign is positive.
This drops the \( 2\sqrt{c} \) term entirely before simplifying.
This is off by one from the correct simplified value.
This is off by one from the correct simplified value.
This is off by two from the correct simplified value.
